title: Permission error when creating files in mounted volume
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**
I have a container which mounts a volume at runtime (using -v). I create a database and migration files in the container as user with uid > 10000. My host uid is < 10000. Therefore, I am having permission errors when those files are created. I am aware that setting my directory permissions to 777 would solve the problem but I'd rather not do that. I also know that I can use `--privileged` when I run a command in the container but this will just delay the problem...

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**
1. The command to run the container:
```
podman run -d \
    --name $container_name \
    --user $(id -u) \
    -v $(pwd)/api:/api \
    -v $(pwd)/data:/var/lib/sqlite \
    $custom_container
```
2. Then I execute some commands in the container, namely:
```
podman exec --user $(id -u) $container_name flask db init
podman exec --user $(id -u) $container_name flask db migrate
```
**Describe the results you received:**
Fails when trying to write in the current directory
`PermissionError: [Errno 13] Permission denied: 'migrations'`

**Describe the results you expected:**
If specifying my uid, I should not have permission errors

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**
My container was build with my uid and runs as my uid, it works thus fine with docker.
**Output of `podman version`:**
```
Version:            1.4.4
RemoteAPI Version:  1
Go Version:         go1.12.6
OS/Arch:            linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```

```
$ uname -a

comments.body:
I assume you're running Podman as non-root?

If so, you might want to try removing the `--user` part of your command line. There are a few security benefits to running applications in rootless containers as UIDs other than 0, but they're not really that significant. UID 0 in the rootless container is the user that launched Podman, not real root on the system - it's not a privileged user, and has no permissions on the host that the user launching Podman did not have.

Alternatively, you can add `--userns=keep-id` and keep `--user`. This will map the UID running Podman so that the UID in the container matches the UID on the host.

> I assume you're running Podman as non-root?

Yes of course, all point of using podman ;-)

I rebuilt the container without creating a user, meaning that the container will run as root from now on. I also removed all mentions of `--user` in the run/exec commands. This seemed to solve the problem. The key was indeed NOT to create a user in my Dockerfile so that the container runs as root. Since I like to have the option to use both docker and podman, I will try the `--userns` option as well.

Thank you very much for your answer !
I am closing this issue as this is not a bug
